title: Children's Privacy under DPbD
description: Children's privacy provisions split between (a) issuedRequirements — what the JPDP DPbD Guideline (April 2026) actually obligates, and (b) analystGuidance — leading-practice content from PCP No. 2/2025 that did NOT survive into the issued text. Mark findings carefully — do not cite the JPDP guideline for obligations the issued text does not contain.

guidelineStatus.issuedReference: Garis Panduan Mereka Bentuk Berdasarkan Perlindungan Data (DPbD), JPDP April 2026, Sections 5.3 (General), 10.2 (Data Integrity), 11.2 (Access)
guidelineStatus.criticalDivergence: PCP No. 2/2025 had a comprehensive standalone children's privacy chapter [D] (Paras 2.38–2.57) including: scope test (likely accessed by children), best-interests-of-child analysis (UN CRC Article 3), 8 age-verification mechanisms, anti-lockout principle, and a PROHIBITION on profiling/ADM of children for advertising/marketing. Almost none of this is in the issued guideline. Children are addressed in only three short paragraphs.

issuedRequirements.description: These are the only children-specific requirements in the issued JPDP DPbD Guideline. Use these for findings citing the issued guideline.

rules.rule: DPbD on the General Principle requires data controllers to apply privacy considerations to data of subjects under 18 years old, ensuring valid consent is obtained on behalf of such subjects from the parent, guardian, or person with parental responsibility.
rules.bmQuote: Pendekatan DPbD dalam pematuhan Prinsip Am juga menghendaki pengawal data untuk menerapkan pertimbangan privasi terhadap data peribadi subjek data yang berumur di bawah lapan belas (18) tahun dengan memastikan persetujuan yang sah diperoleh bagi pihak subjek data tersebut. Persetujuan… hendaklah diperoleh daripada ibu bapa, penjaga atau seseorang yang mempunyai tanggungjawab ibu bapa…

rules.rule: Correction mechanisms must be easily usable by parents/guardians/persons with parental responsibility for under-18 data subjects.
Access mechanisms must be easily usable by parents/guardians/persons with parental responsibility for under-18 data subjects.

analystGuidance.description: Content below is from PCP No. 2/2025 (March 2025 consultation paper) that did NOT survive into the issued April 2026 guideline. Retained here as leading practice / cross-jurisdictional alignment, but MUST NOT be cited as JPDP-issued requirements. May reappear in a future Tata Amalan / Code of Practice for children's data — monitor JPDP announcements.

scope.appliesTo: (PCP-derived) Providers of products or services directed at, intended for, or likely to be accessed by children — including offline (educational, sports, social clubs, health/social support) and digital (websites, apps, IoT, social media, gaming, entertainment, advocacy).
scope.scopeNote: Covers services that a significant number of children use in reality, even if not primarily intended for children.
scope.status: PCP-only; not in issued JPDP guideline

bestInterestsOfChild.principle: (PCP-derived) Data controllers must take into account the best interests of children as a primary consideration where any conflict arises. Source: Article 3 of the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child.
considerations: Keep children safe from exploitation (commercial, sexual, abuse)
Protect and support health and wellbeing
Protect and support physical, psychological, emotional development
Protect children's need to develop their own views and identity
Protect freedom of association and play
Support the needs of children with disabilities
Recognise the role of parents in protecting and promoting children's best interests
Recognise the evolving capacity of the child to form their own view
bestInterestsOfChild.status: PCP-only; not in issued JPDP guideline. UN CRC Article 3 is not cited in the issued guideline.

ageVerification.obligation: (PCP-derived) Data controllers should make reasonable efforts to verify they have received consent to process children's personal data; in higher-risk circumstances this entails establishing age verification mechanisms.

mechanisms.note: Most common but easily bypassed; suitable only for low-risk processing.
Used by e-commerce sites for adult products. Cannot ascertain card user is owner; minimum credit card age varies by country.
AI-powered. Accuracy issues, privacy concerns due to sensitive data use, risk of profiling.
Browsing history, maturity questionnaires, online content/purchase analysis.
Scratch cards or in-situ ID checks at retail outlets.
Traditional ID document checks; for higher-risk: proof of ID; for lower-risk: confirmation email.
Third party (not parent) confirms child's age.
State-issued tools (China, Canada, Australia, EU digital identity wallet).

ageVerification.antiLockoutPrinciple: (PCP-derived) Compliance does not justify locking children out of services or providing inferior two-tier offerings. Doing so risks driving children to lie about their age and access services without child-appropriate protections.
ageVerification.status: PCP-only; not in issued JPDP guideline.

dpbdElementsForChildren.language: Clear, concise, child-friendly language explaining what is done with their personal data.
dpbdElementsForChildren.accessibility: Information in obvious, easy-to-find places. Not in tiny writing. Not designed to nudge acceptance.
dpbdElementsForChildren.context: Just-in-time notifications when data is shared (e.g., before posting, before changing default privacy settings).
dpbdElementsForChildren.parentalDashboard: Where appropriate, provide parents with overall view of activity and settings.
dpbdElementsForChildren.status: PCP-derived leading practice. Not all elements are in the issued JPDP guideline.

prohibitionOnProfiling.rule: (PCP-derived, NOT in issued guideline) Data controllers must not profile children, engage in automated decision-making concerning children, or use their personal data for advertising/marketing purposes — unless they can clearly demonstrate how and why it is in the best interests of children to do so.
prohibitionOnProfiling.criticalNote: This is a load-bearing rule that did NOT survive into the April 2026 issued guideline. The April 2026 issued ADMP Guideline also does not address children. Treat as cross-jurisdictional best practice (GDPR Recital 38; UK ICO Children's Code; EU DSA Article 28). DO NOT cite the JPDP DPbD or ADMP Guidelines for this prohibition.
prohibitionOnProfiling.status: PCP-only; not in issued JPDP guideline.
